ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for almond paste dessert soup; closest match is the general almond food product class. Specific almond paste soup form is not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for almond paste dessert soup; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of almond paste dessert soup is approximately 200 g (one bowl), as commonly served in Singaporean and Chinese dessert stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Almond Paste — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Almond Paste (F02050001). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, MUFA, PUFA, potassium, and vitamin E are supplementary estimates from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (blanched almonds, NDB 12062), scaled to the diluted paste composition, and are clearly documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Almonds and Almond-Based Products — University of Sydney GI Database; supplemented by Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for almond paste dessert soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated from the closest analogous food: almond-based products (whole almonds GI ~0–15; almond milk GI ~25; sweetened almond desserts estimated ~30–40). The added sugar (6.9 g/100g) and dilution with water raise the effective GI above pure almonds. A conservative estimate of 35 (low GI) is applied. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 8.8 × 2) / 100 = 6.2 g. Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content is low at 12 mg/100g; however, added sugar (6.9 g/100g) with low fibre (0.6 g/100g) and low potassium (~80 mg/100g) raises a secondary concern for cardiovascular metabolic load.
hypertension.guidance: Almond paste is low in sodium and generally suitable for hypertension management, but portion size should be controlled given the added sugar content and low potassium-to-sodium ratio; prefer unsweetened or lightly sweetened preparations.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content is 6.9 g/100g with a low estimated GI of ~35; MUFA-dominant fat from almonds moderates glycaemic response, but cumulative sugar intake from larger portions or frequent consumption requires monitoring.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Almond paste has a low estimated glycaemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (8.8 g/100g), making it acceptable in small portions for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided total daily sugar and carbohydrate budgets are respected.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fat profile is MUFA-dominant (approximately 2.7 g/100g monounsaturated fat) with low saturated fat (0.4 g/100g) and no trans fat; almonds are associated with favourable lipid outcomes in peer-reviewed literature.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The MUFA-dominant fat profile and vitamin E content (~3.5 mg/100g) of almond paste support cardiovascular health; saturated fat is low at 0.4 g/100g, and the food is suitable in moderate portions as part of a heart-healthy diet, with attention to added sugar intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: MUFA-dominant fat and vitamin E content provide anti-inflammatory properties; added sugar at 6.9 g/100g contributes a mild pro-inflammatory load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Almond paste is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its MUFA-dominant fat profile and vitamin E content; choose lower-sugar or unsweetened preparations to minimise pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Almond paste (杏仁糊, Xing Ren Hu) is a smooth, thick Chinese dessert soup made from ground blanched almonds, sugar, and egg whites. It is a traditional sweet soup commonly served warm in Singaporean and Chinese dessert stalls and restaurants. The texture is silky and creamy, with a mild, nutty almond flavour and a gentle sweetness from added sugar. Nutritionally, almond paste provides 82 kcal per 100 g, with 2.1 g protein, 4.3 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated, approximately 2.7 g/100g), and 8.8 g carbohydrate including 6.9 g sugar. Dietary fibre is low at 0.6 g/100g and sodium is low at 12 mg/100g. The fat profile mirrors that of whole almonds, with low saturated fat (0.4 g/100g) and meaningful vitamin E content (~3.5 mg/100g), supporting antioxidant and cardiovascular benefits. The estimated glycaemic index is approximately 35 (low), making it a moderate-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions. It is suitable for hypertension due to its low sodium content, though the added sugar and low potassium should be noted. Almond paste is vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free, but contains tree nut (almond) and egg allergens. It is commonly consumed as a dessert or snack and is a staple of traditional Chinese sweet soup culture across Singapore, Malaysia, and southern China.
common_user_queries: Is almond paste good for diabetics?
Is almond paste suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F02050001) at confidence 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source. Saturated fat, MUFA, PUFA, and vitamin E values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central almond composition data (USDA SR Legacy, Almonds, blanched) and are clearly documented as supplementary estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.4 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.7 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.0 g/100g), and vitamin_e_mg (3.5 mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for blanched almonds (NDB 12062), scaled to the diluted almond paste composition reported by HPB. These values are supplementary estimates and not directly reported in the HPB Foodo record. 2. potassium_mg (~80 mg/100g) is estimated from USDA almond composition scaled to the diluted paste; not directly reported in HPB. 3. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific almond paste dessert soup preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database (F02050001) or USDA FoodData Central for this exact diluted product form as of 2026. Limited published data exist for this traditional preparation. Sources consulted: HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) and USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) — neither reports these micronutrients for the specific almond paste soup formulation. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100g based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the traditional preparation (blanched almonds, sugar, egg whites). 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for almond paste dessert soup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 6. Multilingual naming: this food is classified as a Singaporean/Chinese dessert. Chinese name (杏仁糊) is the primary local-language identity. Malay local name 'Pes Badam' is the standard Malay translation used in Singapore food labelling contexts. Tamil local name 'பாதாம் பேஸ்ட்' (badam paste) and Hindi local name 'बादाम का पेस्ट' (badam ka paste) are the standard translational equivalents used in Singapore's multilingual food context; these are translational descriptors rather than culturally native dish names, as this dessert is primarily of Chinese origin.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Almond Paste', a precise canonical English name. Chinese local name 杏仁糊, Malay 'Pes Badam', Tamil 'பாதாம் பேஸ்ட்', and Hindi 'बादाம का पेस्ट' are all present in local_names. Romanized Xing Ren Hu is present. Cuisine is correctly identified as Chinese/Singaporean. All mandatory Singaporean-dish language fields are populated.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (82.0), protein_g (2.1), fat_g (4.3), carbohydrate_g (8.8) are all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (F02050001) at confidence 0.95. nutrient_source is fully documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.6), sodium_mg (12.0), saturated_fat_g (0.4), and sugar_g (6.9) are all present as numeric values. saturated_fat_g is a documented supplementary estimate from USDA SR Legacy.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (35), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (6.2), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), clearly documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodEx2 code A00GG ('Almond paste') is a defensible exact match. FoodOn URI is an approximate match ('closest_parent') with gap note. SNOMED CT is null with honest gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and meal_context are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) are present with concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: All strings in aliases_by_language are present verbatim in the flat aliases array: English variants, Chinese 杏仁糊, Malay 'Pes Badam', Tamil 'பாதாம் பேஸ்ட்' and 'பாதாம் விழுது', Hindi 'बादाம का पेस्ट' and 'बादாம पेस्ट', and romanized 'Xing Ren Hu'. Consistency check passed. Mandatory language alias coverage for Singaporean dish is complete.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft created from input JSON.
Iteration 2: Added Chinese local names (杏仁糊), romanized (Xing Ren Hu), corrected foodon_match_type, documented -1 fields in notes.

Iteration 3 (this iteration): Added Malay local name 'Pes Badam', Tamil local name 'பாதாம் பேஸ்ட்', Hindi local name 'बादाम का पेस்ட' to local_names to satisfy g6 multilingual naming compliance for Singaporean dish. Added all new local-language names and their variants to flat aliases array and aliases_by_language to satisfy q8. Expanded notes to explicitly document every -1 field with source consulted and reason for unavailability, satisfying g3 numeric hygiene. Self-check: all aliases_by_language strings verified present verbatim in flat aliases array.
